speaker: interviewee
text: This tape?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. So in this tape-- this is the fourth tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to cut it short?

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's finish Auschwitz and go to Golleschau. Four.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't forget, I made a tape-- I have a six-hour tape, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. No. We can't do six.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I'm just saying-- Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. So this is 4. So maybe we'll do Auschwitz and Golleschau. It's Tape 4. Phyllis Dreazen talking with Allen Caine in Philadelphia. October 23rd, 1995. We're speaking in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Life was unbearable. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was hungry all the time. I went over one time to the kitchen. I looked around. Maybe I'll find a scrap. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I saw-- I saw a truck pulling up to the kitchen. They were unloading potatoes. Instead of unloading them with forks, they were unloading them with a shovel, right into the pot, the manure and everything with it. And that's how they cooked. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The next day a shipment of 500 German Jews came, but they died within a week from the food. They-- they noticed that the food has bugs in it, manure in it. Of course, it was cooked. Everything was cooked.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I was assigned to do a-- pulling a road wagon, they called it, to pull a wagon. Anything to pull, we-- we used-- they used us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight. And we had a kapo with us, too. After-- a day later, two kapos came over and looking around, and take me out to another group. There was a couple hundred people in the group. They called them crematorium [GERMAN]. By then, I knew what crematorium means, what that means, crematorium, because in Auschwitz they had a little crematorium where they were burning people. They picked me up. I'm walking with them. They put me in a line. I turned around. I walked back to my group. I get-- I take a look. They're after me. They gave me beatings. It wasn't so bad. And again, they take me into there, and again I ran away. Well, the third time, it was-- it was too bad. They already carried me. They beat me up so bad that I couldn't see my eyes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took me to-- I walked with them to the crematorium, number 1, and they make me do was pick up five bricks in my coat, run a half a mile, drop them, and then put them back, and run again. I did that for a half a day. Finally, they disappeared. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went inside. I look around the ovens. The name of the ovens were Top, Topf. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I noticed my brother, my oldest brother works there. He was tamping in the floor. I go over to him. I said, Faigel, I didn't know you're working here. He looks at me. He said, who you? I said, this is Anschel. Don't you recognize me? He says, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: My face was swollen, like my eyes were like pinheads. I couldn't see nothing. We looked at each other. I didn't know he-- he works there. Well, we slept together. Nobody ever spoke what we doing. One brother, we already lost.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you lose him?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one that went to the-- to the sonderkommando.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Anyway, after I worked there, before we went home, the same two kapos that beat me up came over, took my number down. He says, tomorrow, you'll come back here. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I says, Never, never in our-- in your life will I be back here. As we were walking to the barracks, they were grabbing people. I didn't know what for. And I just walked right into their hands. In the big group, I noticed two of my uncles, one-- one of my brothers, one of my brother, Chaim, and a lot of people from my hometown, young guys, strong guys. They gave us this slice of bread. And they took us into Auschwitz. We walked into Auschwitz. Gave us a shower. We slept overnight. Next day, took us in a truck and took us into-- to Golleschau.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in Auschwitz, how long was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: In those six weeks, did you ever hear of any ways of evading, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] going into lying or hiding from the captors or--

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I got friendly with a-- a man. He asked me what I was doing-- what I was doing as a free man. I said, I wasn't-- I'm only a kid. I was-- I-- because all Jews are-- they're bringing all the Jews in here. He says, No. No. Why should they bring here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, then what are you doing? What were you doing? He said, I'm a thief. He's a professional thief.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, come on, buddy, I'll-- I'll show you ways to get away from work. He said, it's hundreds of acres. You could hide if you knew how.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was raining. Takes me into the-- latrine. It's a big latrine. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we stayed there one night, one day. We just went back for roll call.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next day, we hear-- it has two doors. We hear SS is coming. I never believed that they so dumb. Instead of coming from both ends, they all came in from one side, and we came out the other side. They caught about 10 people. They dumped them in the water, in the bathroom, in the toilet. It was very deep. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I didn't go there no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anybody who either took people off the trucks or took people out of the gas chambers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That-- that's-- as far as I know, one woman-- they made a mistake-- she was Gentile. They took her out. That was-- everybody heard about that. One day they brought in 500-and-some women, all women, Jewish women from Warsaw. Half of them were actresses, beautiful-looking women. And the SS-- they were inside before-- they had to get undressed before the gas chamber. And one SS man walks around inside with a whip. That was their way they were walking around. He goes over to one woman. He looks at her.

speaker: interviewee
text: He touches-- with a whip, he touches her breast. He says, how you like that? Our fuhrer made a good choice. Would I be able to do to you what I do it now? You know, he was teasing her and everything, making fun of her.

speaker: interviewee
text: As he was turning around, there were about five SS inside. As he was turning around-- she had a lot of guts-- grabbed his gun, because wherever they went, their-- their holster was always open, ready. She grabbed at his gun, pulled it out, shot him dead. They got so excited outside-- we're surrounded with guards-- that they start shooting through the windows, even to kill their own people, too. Nobody got out alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever hear that any sonderkommandos [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] took their own family to be burned?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I left, after-- after the liberation-- I'll go-- I'll go-- I'll go further, too far away. After the liberation, I was in-- I heard-- I was in Modena, Italy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I know I heard that one man survived the death march and being in-- in the sonderkommando. It was unusual. They promised-- they were walking around among us. They promised bread. They promised anything, so he should show his face. He knew right away what would happen to him. And he got liberated. I went to Italy. And I found out there is a man going to speak to reporters about the whole thing, about what he went through in the-- in the-- as a sonderkommando man. I went there. I recognized him. I knew him. I just wanted to know about my brother, what happened to my brother. I went in there. There was a nurse, a doctor, British reporters, American reporters, Italian-- all kind of reporters. And the men was shaking, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was smoking. He was eating the cigarettes. I went over to him. I asked him why he's doing that, making the whole fuss about this. He said, I got to get it off my chest. He was telling them, while he was burning the people, his next was to open the gas chamber, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to open the gas chamber [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and get the bodies out of there to-- for burning them. As he opened the double door, the bodies usually didn't fall out. He had to pull them together because they were clamped together. As he looked, he collapsed. He saw his two children and his wife [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] among the-- the dead bodies in the gas chamber. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They ran. They picked him up. They put him aside so the kapo and the SS men doesn't see it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then he relaxed. The reason how come that he didn't came with his family, because he must have done something wrong that they put him to-- first they put him in jail and then they brought him to Auschwitz. And that's what-- that's why his family came later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in Auschwitz for six weeks. That's nightmares for the rest of your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: The rest of my life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I've seen too much. I've seen a wagon full of naked people. They used to call their names, who they are. Tell my son they're bringing me-- I'm going to the gas chamber. If you see my wife, tell my wife. We used to see women. I went-- one time I went to work. And I noticed from far away an aunt-- an aunt of mine is walking there. She looked like a man, a long coat, no hair. They sent her hair to Germany to make dolls and mattresses. As she-- When she saw me, she started to cry. I started to cry when I saw her. That was a sister of my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had a kapo, a woman. And she noticed that we're meeting eyes. She ran over to me. She beat me up so bad that I almost spit my teeth out, almost. [GERMAN]. That was-- you ever heard that word, [GERMAN]? That's Muslim. Don't you know you're not allowed to talk to women? I said, I didn't talk to nobody. There's no use. She kept beating, beating me, beating me. And that's the only time I saw my aunt. What was your worst moment in Auschwitz? [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] after they gave me the number, the guy told me, we'll make a cake of soap. I wanted to know what's-- what he's talking about. I felt pretty-- I went over to him. He was a Frenchman.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said to him, what were you mentioning about soap? He says, yes, they make the soap out of people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I couldn't believe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: They made it for about a few days. Later on, they had problems with the fire, so they were putting diesel on the fire. Once they put diesel on the fire, they stopped making the soap. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When the-- when the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] crematorium was in-- in gear, they used to clean the chimney from the crematorium. They had eight inches of fat on the-- in the chimneys. But they didn't make-- they just throw that out. They didn't make. When they got done throwing the ashes in that little river, they used to take it to the farmers. That's why their grass is still green since then. They threw them on the field, the ashes. Human ashes, they threw it on the field.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now let me bring you back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took you. They cleaned you up. They fed you. They put you to sleep in Auschwitz I. And then they took you to Golleschau.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was a subcamp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was 90 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which direction?

speaker: interviewee
text: Toward the Czechoslovakian border.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got there. Right away, they separated my brother. They put him for [GERMAN], they called it, breaking stones. Me, they put in a different place. And I used to come home like 2 o'clock in the-- already in the morning. I went over to my brother. He was just going to work. And we cried. He says, I work hard. I'm breaking stones. And I told him, I'm doing the same thing. The beatings, terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many guys were in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few thousand. That was a working camp. We had a little hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the food any better?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what did they give you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us a couple potatoes, you could see, not peeled, just like that. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] once in a while, they gave you like [GERMAN], an extra piece of bread, an extra little bit of soup. One time they put out the soup to get cool. And I noticed that. I grabbed a pan and I dumped it and I ran away. And the-- the cook saw my face. And there were 2,000 people at that time in a mess hall. And I put the [GERMAN] right on the floor and I mingled with the rest of the guys. My brother looked like me. We're different, but we look like twins. They got a hold of my brother. They beat him up so bad. And he wouldn't say-- he said, I didn't do it. And  he wouldn't say that I did it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, next day, I was standing with my brother. The cook goes by and he sees us both. He looked. He looked. He says, now I know. You did it. But he didn't touch me.

speaker: interviewee
text: He became-- he got respect for us. Why? Because a father and son used to have fist fights, two brothers used to steal from each other. And here, my brother got beaten up and he wouldn't say-- I would share that with him anyway. And a year later, they took him in the kitchen, made him a cook for that. Do you think that the reason, or part of the reason, you survived was because the two of you were together?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little bit, yes. Yes, a little bit. And I was the best worker, the youngest and the best worker in the place. I come from work one time. I see-- I hear somebody moaning, moaning. I'm looking around. I can't find it. Finally, between the beds-- there was two floors, 2,00 people sleeping in one sleeping quarters. And I see a guy who's trying to hang himself. But he was a big guy, a Czechoslovakian. I knew him. Eric was his name. But he was too big. When he put the piece of wire on his neck-- so he couldn't-- he reached the floor. Well, he couldn't do nothing. But he already hurt himself when he was kneeling, and he cut already a little bit his throat. He-- and I found him. He sees me. He says, Anschel, help me. Help me. I want to die. Help me. So I couldn't pick him up. I was going to help him untie. But I couldn't pick him up. He was a big guy. So I saw a kapo. I said to the kapo, Herr Kapo, come help me get the guy off. He walked up on the bed. He jumped on his shoulder 'til he cut off-- cut practically half of his neck off. He said, That's how you do it. That's how you save a life. This was the worst place. The beatings down there. It was a small place. You couldn't get away with getting beatings, beatings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work in the camp or did you go out to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of times I go-- most of the time, I go out.

speaker: interviewee
text: What was the weather like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The weather was unbelievable. It was always cold, always raining, and always below zero. That's in the mountains, high in the mountains. That's part of the Carpathi. And we used to pull the stones off. One time, I got sick. I got typhoid. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was the first few days from the typhoid. And I was in [GERMAN], sick bay. And I hear Mengele is here. He was like a vulture. He heard there's an epidemic of typhoid, he was right there. Took your number down. That's it. [GERMAN]-- special, take care.

speaker: interviewee
text: He comes in. The first thing I was-- like his eyes were right on me. What is that guy doing here? The-- the doctor jumps, salutes, and calls his number out and he tells him what's wrong with me. He didn't say it's typhoid.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says to the doctor, listen to me. In five minutes, I'll be back. If that guy is still here, I'll taking him to heaven. He's going to heaven.

speaker: interviewer
text: As he walks out, he says, you better get dressed. Get out of here. I said, wait a minute. What number do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, 73,000. This is the guy they took out the dead house. This is the doctor. And this is the doctor that I put-- he was standing in front of me with a big stomach, and I put it-- sew his number is on. They sent him away. To get rid of him, they sent him to Golleschau. He was such a nice person, such a nice person. And I told him about it. We both cried. And I reminded him, I was the one that put the-- sew the number on you. But later on, he got-- they-- they shot him. He got shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ever do any sabotage in Golleschau?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We-- one time, we got a guy from-- a Russian guy, a Jew. He was in the KBG. But nobody should known about it. He was from Bialystok. And he says to me-- because everybody knew me, I was popular-- if I would bring in a gun if somebody gave me. We wanted to build something up. I said, if somebody give me, I'll bring it in. I can do. Nobody will check me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why am I talking like that? One time, Red Cross, International Red Cross came around. He was walking around, walking around. He taps me on the shoulder. He wants to talk to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: He asked me how they treat me here, how-- what kind of work I do, what kind of food I get. In back of him stays the commandant. He looks at me like this. I told him everything. He's a civilian from Switzerland-- from Sweden, Red Cross. And after he left, they used to give us milk-- the youngsters get milk. And the second time he came around, he was looking for me already, so they wouldn't dare lay a hand to me. So I could help my friends, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you strong?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very strong. Very strong. Like I once clogged up-- I stopped the whole factory. And here a-- a man in uniform comes the next day. I see my wagon, the one I'm loading the stones, is marked with a cross a red cross. He comes in. I say, uh-oh. So I said to my brother, trouble is coming. We-- we're in trouble.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says to me, hey, you two brothers. He knew us. We were the best workers. Did anybody helped you load the stones last night? I said, no. How did you put in there stones? The opening where the stones getting dropped in, they were bigger than the opening. And a stone like this weighs 400 pounds. So that's how you stopped the factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had nothing to answer him. He said, that's sabotage, and you get shot for sabotage. We just lowered our heads and were just standing. Everybody worked four guys. We worked two guys. He said, listen, you two brothers. Don't do that again. Once more, and I'll forget about who you are and what you are. You'll get shot. And we thanked him. And that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your closest call, the closest you came to getting killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We came to work one time and here is snow came down during the night. Can't find nothing. And it's up to my belly button, the snow. Can't do nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's all I had on my shoes was-- I don't have it with me, that things. I wore Holland shoes, wooden shoes. Clugs? Clogs? Clogs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Clogs. I tie up-- I tied them up, and that's how I walked in the snow with them. Later on, I took them off and I walked in the snow barefooted. That's why my toes are frostbitten.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the guy, the kapo comes over. He says, aren't you guys going to go to work? So one guy, a Frenchman, says no. OK. [GERMAN]. In line. March, march. Running back and forth, gymnastics.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was getting deep in the snow. A half a mile-- I couldn't run. My brother and I, we stopped, and just plainly, we hugged each other and we stayed. We're waiting for the Angel of Death to come and get us. And we looked from the side. He killed six people that time. It was a Pole, a Christian Pole. He was a kapo. And he killed six people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of a sudden, he looks at us. Aren't you guys working? Nobody answers him. Aren't you going to run? Nobody answers him. He comes over and he picks up that handle from a shovel, from a fork. And I said to my brother, remember, my grandmother said my mom will watch over us? She did. He never lowered it. How come?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. I asked me-- we asked-- my brother and I, we used to talk about it. He asked me. I asked him. I don't know why. He picked it up and he was going to lower that thing. But he lowered it not onto us. We just had our heads very low, holding each other. And he never-- never hurt us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe he already had his quota.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't think so. I don't think so. He just didn't. I don't know. I-- that-- that only part, I believe in a miracle. Anything else, I don't believe in a miracle. Nothing. Only that part, I believe in a miracle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe one miracle is enough.
